ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Punjabi Kali Dal or whole black urad dal curry; closest parent 'lentil food product' (FOODON_03311434) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific term for Punjabi Kali Dal; A00LC (Legumes and pulses, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Punjabi Kali Dal or whole black urad dal curry as a prepared dish.

main_ingredients: whole black urad lentils (sabut urad dal)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Punjabi Kali Dal is approximately 250 g, equivalent to one medium bowl as typically served in an Indian restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Black gram whole (cooked dal preparation), ICMR-NIN
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) for cooked whole black gram (sabut urad dal) preparation. Values reflect the cooked dish as served, accounting for water absorption during cooking and typical tempering with oil/ghee, ginger, garlic, and cumin. The restaurant version (Maharani Table, Singapore) may include additional ghee or cream; values represent a standard home-style preparation. USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, FDC ID 175237, 'Lentils, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, without salt') was used as a cross-reference for secondary micronutrients not reported in ICMR-NIN.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Black gram (urad dal, whole, cooked)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 30 is based on whole black gram (sabut urad dal, cooked) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care). Whole black urad lentils have a consistently low GI (range 26–38) due to high resistant starch, dietary fibre, and protein content. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 33.75) / 100 = 10.1, using 250 g serving size and 13.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from tempering and added salt
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g restaurant serving of Punjabi Kali Dal provides approximately 525 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and monitor cumulative daily sodium intake, keeping total intake below 2000 mg per day as per WHO guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 30) and high dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Punjabi Kali Dal has a low glycemic index of approximately 30 and a glycemic load of about 10 per serving, making it a suitable legume-based dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal with controlled total carbohydrate intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary fibre, plant protein, and low saturated fat in standard preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Punjabi Kali Dal provides approximately 9.5 g dietary fibre and 18 g plant protein per 250 g serving, supporting LDL cholesterol reduction; however, restaurant versions may include additional ghee or cream that increases saturated fat, and patients with cardiovascular disease should confirm preparation method before consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Punjabi Kali Dal is a traditional North Indian legume dish made from whole black urad lentils (sabut urad dal, Vigna mungo) that are slow-cooked until tender and then tempered (tadka) with hot oil or ghee infused with cumin seeds, ginger, garlic, onion, and tomato. The dish originates from the Punjab region of the Indian subcontinent and is a staple of Punjabi home cooking and restaurant menus across India, Singapore, and the broader South Asian diaspora. The texture is thick and hearty, with a creamy mouthfeel derived from the starch released by the lentils during prolonged cooking. The flavour profile is earthy, mildly spiced, and savoury, with aromatic notes from cumin and ginger. Nutritionally, Punjabi Kali Dal is a rich source of plant protein (approximately 7.2 g per 100 g cooked), dietary fibre (3.8 g per 100 g), iron, folate, potassium, and magnesium. Its low glycemic index (approximately 30) makes it particularly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The dish is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian, though it may contain ghee (clarified butter) and is therefore not vegan in most restaurant preparations. In Singapore, it is served at Indian restaurants such as Maharani Table at Boat Quay, typically as a side dish with naan, roti, or rice. Clinically, the high fibre and plant protein content support cardiovascular health and glycaemic control, while the sodium content from added salt and tempering requires monitoring for individuals with hypertension.
common_user_queries: Is Kali Dal good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked whole black gram data, which is the closest approved primary source for this dish. No exact entry for 'Punjabi Kali Dal' as a prepared restaurant dish exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo. Values reflect a standard home-style preparation and may differ from the restaurant version at Maharani Table, which may include additional ghee or cream. GI is based on analogous whole black gram data from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this food category and is not expected to be present in a plant-based legume dish. 2. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for cooked whole black gram. 3. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish containing no animal-derived ingredients in its base form; confirmed zero for plant legumes per ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy. 4. Trans fat is 0.0; confirmed negligible/zero for home-style preparation without hydrogenated fats per USDA SR Legacy cross-reference. 5. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' is preserved from the input record as legumes are classified under this group in the Singapore HPB food grouping system. 6. The cuisine field is set to 'Indian' reflecting the true culinary origin of the dish; the input record listed 'Singapore' as the source market context (field survey data), not the cuisine origin. 7. Dietary flag is_vegan is set to false as the dish typically contains ghee in restaurant preparation; is_dairy_free is set to false for the same reason. 8. The restaurant version served at Maharani Table - Boat Quay may have higher sodium and fat content than the values reported here; values represent a standard preparation baseline.
